title: Paper clay botanical wall panel
subtitle: A 200 mm by 150 mm slab tile with pressed botanical compositions
excerpt: A rectangular paper clay slab 200 mm by 150 mm with pressed leaf, flower, and seed-head impressions arranged as a composed panel. No kiln needed. Wire-hung and PVA sealed.

glossaryTerms.definition: air-dry clay with cellulose fibres added, which bridge cracks and strengthen thin sections
the stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to carve and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay : fragile and absorbent
unfired clay work at any stage from freshly formed to bone-dry

content.text: This panel is a 200 mm by 150 mm slab of
paper clay
8 mm thick, with pressed impressions of leaves, a flower head, a seed head, and a stem arranged as a composed botanical panel.
Greenware

from plain air-dry clay can crack at thin relief lines where a midrib presses deep; the cellulose fibres in paper clay bridge those micro-fractures, keeping fine botanical detail intact through drying. Two hanging holes are pierced at the top for wire or cord. No kiln is needed.
Preparation

Collect fresh botanicals the day you plan to work: fresh leaves and flowers press more clearly than dried ones because they are still pliable and transfer detail cleanly. A composition of different sizes reads better than a single repeated element. Suggested grouping: two or three leaves of different sizes and shapes (a large simple leaf, a smaller compound leaf, a slender blade), one flower head pressed flat, one seed head or dried capsule, and a thin stem. Arrange the botanicals loosely on the table before rolling to check the composition fits the 200 mm by 150 mm panel. Cut 8 mm spacer guides from card.
Construction
Wedge 400 g of paper clay and press into a rough rectangle on the wooden board. Place the 8 mm spacer cards on either side and roll to an even thickness across the full width of the spacers.

Cut a 200 mm by 150 mm rectangle using the fettling knife and a ruler as a straight edge. The rectangle should be slightly larger than needed : the clay will shrink 5 to 8 mm overall on drying, so cut to 210 mm by 160 mm if you want the finished panel to be close to 200 mm by 150 mm. Smooth the surface lightly with the damp sponge.

Lay the botanical elements onto the clay surface face-down, arranging them as your planned composition. Start with the largest leaf, then position the smaller leaves around it, then add the flower head and seed head. Aim to leave 15 mm clear at each edge and ensure the elements overlap slightly for a coherent composition rather than isolated spots.

Place a sheet of baking paper over the botanicals and roll over the whole surface firmly with the rolling pin. The paper prevents the clay sticking to the rolling pin. Press each botanical in turn through the paper with your fingertips after rolling to confirm full contact, particularly at the tips of thin leaves.

Remove the baking paper. Peel each botanical away from the clay slowly, starting at the tip or a narrow end and working toward the midrib. If a leaf sticks, use the needle tool to release the edge without dragging the impression.

Once the botanicals are removed, check the impressions. Use the needle tool to deepen any part of the impression that is too shallow : trace along the midrib lines and leaf margins if they have not transferred clearly.
When the clay has reached the
leather-hard

stage, pierce two hanging holes 12 mm down from the top edge, spaced 30 mm in from each side. Rotate the needle tool gently to create clean holes approximately 4 mm in diameter. Smooth the hole rims with a damp fingertip.
Drying
Leave flat on the wooden board for 48 to 3 days until
bone-dry

. Large flat panels are prone to warping along the longer dimension. To prevent this, place a flat board over the panel during the first 12 hours of drying and weight it lightly. Remove the weighted board once the panel is past leather-hard and no longer at risk of surface damage from pressure. Paper clay resists corner cracking better than plain air-dry clay, but drying near a localised heat source on one side can still cause differential shrinkage.
Finishing

Seal all surfaces with undiluted PVA, working the brush into the botanical impression recesses. Two coats, each dried clear. Thread 30 cm of picture wire through both hanging holes, twist the ends together at the back, and the panel is ready to hang. Alternatively, apply a dilute acrylic wash in a warm tone over the impressed surface before PVA sealing: this gives the panel a printed-botanical look with the colour concentrated in the impression lines.
Care

Display indoors only. Dust the impressed surface with a dry, soft-bristled brush. Do not hang in a room with high sustained humidity. If a hairline crack appears across a thin impression line after extended display, fill with paper clay slip, dry, and re-seal.
Variations
Seasonal panels: press spring blossom and new leaves for one, autumn leaves and seed pods for another, each as a distinct panel in the same format.
Single-species panel: press multiple impressions of the same plant species at different scales. This creates a repeat botanical study.
Write the plant names in the clay at leather-hard with the needle tool, below each impression, for a herbarium-label style.
Make a smaller 100 mm by 80 mm version with a single leaf impression as a label holder or greeting tile.

rows.problem: Botanicals tear when peeled away from the clay
rows.cause: Fresh plant material pressed too deeply into soft clay
rows.fix: Use the needle tool to work under the botanical edge first, then peel. Press botanicals onto slightly firmer clay: allow the slab to firm for 10 minutes after rolling before pressing.

rows.problem: Impression too faint in thick leaf midribs
rows.cause: The midrib did not make full contact with the clay through the overlying leaf
rows.fix: After rolling, press along the midrib firmly through the baking paper with a fingertip. Deepen with the needle tool once the botanical is removed.

rows.problem: Panel warps along the longer edge
rows.cause: Differential drying : top surface dries faster than the underside
rows.fix: Weight the panel under a board for the first 12 hours of drying. If already warped at bone-dry, do not force flat : display with the warp facing the wall and use a slight tilt from the hanging wire.

rows.cause: Holes pierced when clay too dry, or clay around the hole too thin
rows.fix: Always pierce holes at leather-hard with a slow rotating motion. Ensure at least 12 mm of clay between the hole and the nearest edge.

rows.problem: PVA makes the clay look darker in the impression recesses
rows.cause: PVA pools in deep impression lines and appears wet/dark when thick
rows.fix: Work PVA into recesses with a fine liner brush and wick out any excess. The darkening reduces significantly once the PVA has cured fully clear.
